title: The King Of Comedy Who Quit Acting At The Top — Stephen Chow's Career Still Doesn't Make Any Sense (In The Best Way)
dek: From a rejected TVB audition to the highest-grossing Chinese film ever made, here's how Stephen Chow built (and then walked away from) one of the wildest careers in cinema.

items.heading: He Got Rejected From Acting School — Right Alongside The Guy Who Became Tony Leung
items.text: Stephen Chow's first shot at TVB's famous actor training program ended in rejection while his friend Tony Leung Chiu-wai got in. A year later a neighbor put in a good word, he got a second shot, and this time it stuck — leading straight to his gig as host of the kids' show that made him a household name.

images.caption: Before the box office records, he was a rejected TVB hopeful
The friend who got in on the first try

items.heading: Then He Invented A Whole Genre Of Comedy That's Basically Impossible To Translate
items.text: By the 1990s Chow had perfected 'mo lei tau' — a Cantonese style built on rapid puns, non-sequiturs, and anachronisms so specific to the language that critics still call it untranslatable. It made him a box office king in Hong Kong throughout the '90s, even as Western audiences had no idea who he was yet.

images.caption: The self-styled King of Mo Lei Tau

items.heading: Shaolin Soccer Made Him A Legend At Home — Then Miramax Sat On It For Two Years
items.text: Shaolin Soccer became the highest-grossing domestic film in Hong Kong history and finally caught Hollywood's attention, with Miramax buying it up for U.S. distribution. But the studio cut the film down and shelved it for so long that pirated copies beat the official release to American audiences.

images.caption: The film that finally put Chow on Hollywood's radar

items.heading: Kung Fu Hustle Then Blew Past That Record — With Sony's Money Behind It
items.text: After the Shaolin Soccer stumble, Sony Pictures Classics backed Chow directly for his next film, and Kung Fu Hustle became the highest-grossing Hong Kong film ever made at the time and the highest-grossing foreign-language film in North America in 2005. Bill Murray called it 'the supreme achievement of the modern age in terms of comedy.'

images.caption: Sony's bet on Chow paid off big

items.heading: He Was Set To Direct AND Star In The Green Hornet — Then Walked Away From Both
items.text: In 2008, Columbia Pictures signed Chow to direct The Green Hornet and play Kato, the role made famous by Bruce Lee. He dropped the directing job over creative differences to make time for a Jack Black superhero comedy instead, then exited the acting role entirely months later — the part eventually went to Jay Chou.

images.caption: The role — and director's chair — Chow ultimately gave up

items.heading: The Mermaid Became The Highest-Grossing Chinese Film Of All Time — In About Two Weeks
items.text: Chow's 2016 fantasy comedy The Mermaid smashed China's opening records and crossed $400 million in under three weeks, dethroning Monster Hunt to become the highest-grossing film in Chinese box office history, a title it held from 2016 to 2019.

images.caption: China's all-time box office champion, briefly

items.heading: He Hasn't Acted On Screen Since 2008 — And Says He's Not Coming Back
items.text: CJ7 was Chow's last on-screen performance; after that he moved entirely behind the camera, writing, directing, and producing hits like Journey to the West: Conquering the Demons — briefly the highest-grossing Chinese-language film ever — and The New King of Comedy. When asked point-blank about an acting comeback, Chow's answer was simply: "Forget about it."

images.caption: Directing from behind the camera, still breaking records
